_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Progressive abdominal distension, abdominal discomfort, weight gain, early satiety, shortness of breath, dyspnea, or increased abdominal pressure due to fluid accumulation.
Symptoms such as fever, abdominal tenderness, or confusion, suggesting spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
Flank dullness, shifting dullness, a fluid wave, or evidence of pleural effusions on physical examination.

Findings related to underlying causes, such as stigmata of cirrhosis (spider angioma, palmar erythema, abdominal wall collaterals) or advanced liver disease (spider angiomata, jaundice, muscle wasting, gynecomastia, leukonychia).
Homogenous freely mobile anechoic collection in the peritoneal cavity, especially in Morison pouch, on ultrasound.
Diagnosis confirmed by diagnostic abdominal paracentesis with appropriate ascitic fluid analysis (including blood cell count, PMN count, and bacterial culture).

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment
Cancer markers CA-19-9, CEA, CA-125 (females) (as and when applicable)

claim.label: Detailed Indoor Case Papers and Treatment given

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment submitted?
Was the liver function test report submitted?
Was the Ultrasound abdomen/Upper Gl endoscopy report of patient suggestive of ascites?
Was the Cancer markers CA-19-9, CEA, CA-125 (females) (as and when applicable) report submitted?

cpd_claim.q: Was Detailed Indoor case papers and treatment details submitted?
Was the Serum ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) report submitted?
Was the Detailed Discharge Summary submitted with the date of the follow-up mentioned?

common_queries: Clinical notes or USG Abdomen/Upper GI endoscopy reports were not indicative of Ascites.
Liver Function Test report missing at pre-authorization.
Serum Ascites Albumin Gradient (SAAG) report missing at claim submission.
Detailed Discharge Summary missing or lacking date of follow-up at claim submission.
Clinical notes at pre-authorization are incomplete regarding evaluation findings, indications, or planned line of treatment.
Applicable cancer markers (CA-19-9, CEA, CA-125 for females) report not submitted when required.

clinical_key_pointers: Ascites is the pathologic accumulation of fluid within the peritoneal cavity.
It is the most common complication of cirrhosis and occurs in about 50% of patients with decompensated cirrhosis in 10 years.
surgical_options: Surgical debulking and chemotherapy (for ovarian malignancy causing low-albumin-gradient ascites)
management_details: Sodium restriction and diuretics form the basis of treatment.
For high-albumin-gradient ascites in cirrhosis: abstinence from alcohol, dietary sodium restriction to 88 mEq (2000 mg)/day, and diuretics (spironolactone and furosemide in a ratio of 100:40 mg/day).
Patients with treatable liver conditions (autoimmune hepatitis, chronic hepatitis B with reactivation, hemochromatosis, Wilson disease) should receive specific therapy.
Liver transplantation may be a better option for some patients with cirrhosis.
Patients with low-albumin-gradient ascites (e.g., non-ovarian peritoneal carcinomatosis) may benefit from outpatient therapeutic paracentesis.
Patients with ovarian malignancy may benefit from surgical debulking and chemotherapy.
TB peritonitis is treated with anti-tuberculous medications.
Pancreatic ascites and postoperative lymphatic leak from a distal splenorenal shunt or radical lymphadenectomy may resolve spontaneously.
